calendar.matters:
MASTER CALENDAR SHORT CAUSE
Off Calendar Mar-08-2024 Vacated

MOTION FOR SUMMARY JUDGMENT .
Off Calendar Feb-01-2024 Granted Per Written Order
MINI MINUTES:

Appearances (via Zoom videoconference):
Defendant Cesar Viana present;
No other appearances made in this matter.

Hearing RE: PLAINTIFF GOLDMAN SACHS BANK USA's MOTION FOR SUMMARY JUDGMENT.

Matter discussed. The Court notes Defendant failed to comply with local rules regarding contesting a tentative ruling. The Court adopts its tentative ruling.

Plaintiff Goldman Sachs Bank USA's motion for summary judgment is granted.

Pro per defendant Cesar Viana took out a loan from Goldman Sachs and later defaulted on payment. Goldman Sachs sued for the common count of open book account for money due. It now moves for summary judgment, claiming it is entitled to the entire alleged balance of $26,377.69 as a matter of law.

To prevail on summary judgment, the movant must show there is no triable issue of material fact such that it is entitled to judgment as a matter of law. (CCP 437c.) A plaintiff moving for summary judgment bears the burden of persuasion that each element of the cause of action has been proved. (CCP 437c (p)(1).) If shown, the burden shifts to the defendant to demonstrate a triable issue of material fact. (Id.) The court liberally construes evidence in favor of the opponent and resolves doubts in their favor. (Ghazarian v. Magellan Health, Inc. (2020) 53 Cal.App.5th 171, 182.)

Here, the sole cause of action is for open book account. A book account is a detailed statement of debit/credit transactions kept by a creditor in the regular course of business, and in a reasonably permanent manner. (CCP 337(a).) A book account is open where a balance remains due. (Professional Collection Consultants v. Lujan (2018) 23 Cal.App.5th 685, 690-691.) Goldman Sachs established each element of its open book account claim: (1) Viana took out a loan with Goldman Sachs (UMF 1, Decl. Knudtson, Exh. A); (2) an account record of the loan credit/debit transactions exists (UMF 2, Decl. Knudtson, Exh. B, C); Goldman Sachs maintained this record in the ordinary course of business (UMF 7, Decl. Knudtson, para. 4); the account record was kept in a reasonably permanent electronic form (UMF 7, Decl. Knudtson, Exh. B, C); and Viana owes $26,377.69 on this loan. (UMF 10, Decl. Knudtson, Exh. A, B, C.)

As Goldman Sachs has established every element, the burden shifts to Viana to prove a triable issue of material fact exists. In turn, Viana acknowledges the loan's existence but disputes the calculation of interest and fees charged. (Defendant's response, para. 1.) These unsupported statements, however, are insufficient to raise a triable issue of material fact. (Trovato v. Beckman Coulter, Inc. (2011) 192 Cal.App.4th 319, 324.)

Lastly, Viana claims "this motion and action lack proper standing" because Goldman Sachs purportedly failed to identify a specific account number. (Defendant's response, para. 2.) Goldman Sachs does have standing. In any event, Viana already conceded the existence of an open book account with Goldman Sachs, and the motion includes sufficient identifying information, such as the partially redacted account number. (Decl. Knudtson, Exh. B.)

Order signed. Judgment signed.

Judge: Richard B. Ulmer Jr.; Clerk: V. Da Fonseca; Not Reported(302/RBU)
